Case Study · INFO-672 Design Systems · Spring 2026

Pawprint

A design system built to connect animals with loving homes.

Role Manager — Forms, Text Fields, Navigation, Color System, Spacing
Team Debo, Sylvia, Nicole, Zhen
Client Adopt a Pet (adoptapet.com)
Tools Figma · ZeroHeight · Figma Slides
7
Component Categories
50+
UI Components
4
Design Principles
1
Shared Language

01 — The Problem

A product built without
a shared system.

Adopt a Pet is one of the largest pet adoption platforms in the United States, connecting millions of animals with potential adopters every year. But behind its warm mission was a design reality that told a different story: a product built over time, by many hands, with no shared visual language to hold it together.

Our team was asked to do something about it. We would audit the existing site, identify the inconsistencies, and build Pawprint — a design system from the ground up, built specifically for the adoption experience.

My role was Manager. Beyond coordinating the team's weekly check-ins and keeping work on track, I was directly responsible for some of the most foundational pieces of the system: the color tokens, the spacing scale, the Navigation components, and the Forms and Text Fields that users interact with at every critical moment of the adoption journey.

01

Too Many Colors

8+ unrelated colors in use with no naming convention, no token structure, and no accessibility checks — making the brand feel inconsistent from page to page.

02

Broken Typography

Two typefaces used with no clear rules. Over 20 distinct text styles found in the audit — sizes, weights, and fonts applied without any system.

03

No Shared Components

Buttons, cards, forms, and icons built independently by each team member. Same problems, solved differently, every single time.

Interface Inventory — 6 pages, hundreds of UI elements catalogued across Navigation, Color, Typography, Icons, Buttons, Images, Forms, and Blocks

My Responsibilities

Manager

Color System Spacing Scale Grid Navigation Forms Text Fields Find a Pet — Interface Inventory Team Coordination
"Good design can make the difference between a pet found and a pet forgotten." Pawprint Design System — Getting Started

02 — The Process

How we got here.

01

Interface Inventory — Find a Pet

Before designing anything, we had to understand what already existed. Each team member took ownership of a different section of the Adopt a Pet site. I audited the Find a Pet pages — the search results experience where adopters first encounter the animals available near them.

What I found was striking. Across the puppy, kitten, and general pet search variants, the same UI problems appeared in different forms: pet listing cards with inconsistent layouts, search bars with varying styling, color backgrounds that shifted between cyan, yellow, and blue with no clear logic, and no standardized pagination behavior. The page that should feel the most exciting — seeing all the animals waiting for a home — was visually scattered.

Original Adopt a Pet — Find a Pet page. Inconsistent card layouts, unsystematic color use, no standardized pagination.

Takeaway

The audit gave me a clear picture of what the system needed to prioritize. The card and navigation components would need to be airtight — they carried the emotional weight of the entire adoption experience.

02

Building the Foundation — Color, Spacing & Grid

Every design system needs its foundations before its components. I started with the color system, building a two-layer token structure: a primitive layer of raw values (cyan-500, neutral-400) and a semantic layer that mapped those values to purpose (brand-primary, text-secondary, button-default). Changing the brand color in the future would cascade through the entire system automatically.

The spacing scale followed the same logic. An 11-step scale anchored at 4px — from spacing-xs through spacing-6xl at 96px — with semantic names so teammates building other components weren't guessing at values. I also defined the 12-column grid with 80px margins and 24px gutters, anchored to Spacing-5xl and Spacing-xl respectively, so every layout decision had a principled foundation.

Color Guide — Primitive palette organized by ramp and weight

Spacing Scale — 11 steps from 4px to 96px

12-column Grid — 80px margins (Spacing-5xl), 24px gutters (Spacing-xl)

Takeaway

These foundation decisions weren't just my own. When teammates started building Cards and Blocks, they pulled my spacing tokens directly into their frames. Being thorough here meant the whole system aligned without anyone having to manually match values.

03

Navigation — The Top of Every Screen

Navigation is the one component that appears on every single page. Getting it wrong would mean inconsistency everywhere. I built four navigation components: the primary navbar in two states (default and expanded search), the secondary navbar for content categories, breadcrumbs, and pagination.

Pagination required particular care — it needed to communicate context ("1-42 of 4,500 pets available") while remaining usable across hundreds of pages. The primary navbar had to handle two distinct states and integrate the search bar with location and pet type inputs. Every interactive state — default, hover, active, disabled — was fully documented.

Navigation legend — Primary navbar (2 states), Secondary navbar, Breadcrumbs, Pagination with context indicators

Takeaway

Navigation taught me about the weight of shared components. Once a teammate drops your navbar into their frame, your decisions become their constraints. That responsibility made me more deliberate and more communicative about every choice.

04

Forms & Text Fields — Where Trust Is Built

Adoption is a high-stakes decision. The moments where users enter their information — creating an account, submitting a search, signing up for a newsletter — are moments of trust. I designed three form layouts and a complete text field library to serve these moments with care.

The account creation form included an optional pet matching section with radio buttons for pet type, household composition, and current pets. For Text Fields, I documented seven component types — Text box, Email box, Enter email box, Dropdown-Text box, Dropdown, Password Info, and Homepage Location — each with their full range of states. There was a point where I wasn't sure whether form error states should follow existing color tokens or need their own semantic token layer. I deliberated alone for too long before bringing it to the team — a conversation that took ten minutes to settle.

Forms — Create account, Email newsletter, Homepage search

Text Fields — 7 types across all interaction states

Takeaway

Asking questions earlier is faster than solving alone. The best decisions in a systems context aren't made in isolation — they're made in conversation.

03 — The Solution

Pawprint — a shared
language for Adopt a Pet.

The system is built around three core truths: adopters make decisions with their hearts first, shelter staff need tools that are fast and low-friction, and trust is the hardest thing to earn and the easiest to lose.

🎨

Color System

A two-layer token architecture. Cyan-based primary palette with neutral grays. Semantic tokens map primitive values to purpose: brand, text, background, button, error.

Primitive → Semantic
✍️

Typography

Lora for brand headings — warm, serif, trustworthy. Inter for UI text — clean and legible. 14 type styles from Display to Caption, in Basic and Emphasized variants.

Lora + Inter
📐

Spacing & Grid

11-step spacing scale from 4px to 96px. 12-column grid with 80px margins. 7 corner radius values — giving Pawprint its rounded, approachable character.

4px Base Unit

My Components

Four areas built from foundation to full implementation, each serving a specific moment in the adoption experience.

01

Navigation

Finding Your Way

Primary navbar (2 states), secondary navbar, breadcrumbs, and pagination. Every navigation pattern a user encounters on Adopt a Pet — standardized and fully documented.

02

Forms

Moments of Trust

Account creation, email newsletter signup, and homepage search — the three forms where adopters commit their information. Every layout reduces friction and earns confidence.

03

Text Fields

Every State, Covered

Seven field types — Text box, Email box, Enter email box, Dropdown-Text box, Dropdown, Password Info, Homepage Location — each across their full range of interaction states.

04

Color + Spacing + Grid

The System Under the System

The foundations every other component depends on. Two-layer color tokens, 11-step spacing scale, 12-column grid — decisions that cascaded through the entire UI kit.

See It in Action

Every element on these assembled pages is a Pawprint component — built from the same foundations, pulled from the same kit.

Pawprint Design System — UI Kit cover

Homepage — navigation, hero search, pet carousel cards

Search Results — expanded navbar, 4-column pet listing grid

Blog page — secondary navigation, 3-column blog card grid

Four principles.
Every decision.

The design principles we established at the start weren't decoration. They were decision-making tools — a way to resolve ambiguity when the right answer wasn't obvious.

  • Lead with the animal, not the interface. The pet is the hero of every screen.
  • Earn trust at every step. Patterns should reduce anxiety, not create it.
  • Warmth is a design decision. Every surface should feel like it was made by someone who cares.
  • Accessible to every kind of home. WCAG AA compliance is a baseline, not a ceiling.

What I Learned

Systems thinking is
collaborative thinking.

On Collaboration

I didn't expect how much my components would affect everyone else's work. The spacing scale fed directly into Cards and Blocks that teammates were designing simultaneously. Being thorough with my own work wasn't just about quality — it was about not creating friction downstream. Good collaboration isn't just communication. It's being dependable with the work you own.

On Asking for Help

During the Text Fields work, I spent too long trying to resolve whether form error states needed their own semantic token layer before bringing it to the team. The conversation took ten minutes. The internal deliberation took hours. I want to get more comfortable raising questions earlier — especially when the decision seems small but has system-wide implications.

04 — Conclusion

What came out of it,
and what comes next.

Pawprint ended as a fully documented design system: a Figma UI Kit with 50+ components across 7 categories, a ZeroHeight documentation site with contribution guidelines, and a pitch deck delivered to our class as hypothetical internal stakeholders.

The system works because it has principles under it. Every component traces back to one of the four guiding principles established at the beginning — and that made decision-making faster and easier to communicate across the team. The Thursday meeting rhythm held everything together. We never let a missed meeting become a missed week.

If I were to continue, I'd want to test Pawprint against a real product sprint — pulling components into actual design work to see where the system holds and where it breaks. That's where a design system proves itself.

  • 01 Publish the UI Kit to Figma Community — open to all Adopt a Pet designers
  • 02 Launch ZeroHeight documentation with full contribution policy
  • 03 Pilot with one product team to validate component coverage in real workflows
  • 04 Expand to mobile — responsive tokens and mobile-specific component variants

Major Takeaway

A design system isn't a deliverable. It's an agreement. It only works when every person building it trusts that the others are being careful with the parts they own — and communicates when they're not sure. That's the part that can't be documented in ZeroHeight. It has to be practiced.
